title: Third conditional: could have vs would have
question: If I had known about the delay, I ___ a different flight.
explanation: 'Could have + past participle' expresses a past possibility that was available but not taken. The distractors use impossible verb forms: bare infinitive instead of participle, 'had' after a modal, or -ing after 'have'.

title: Third conditional: might have
question: If the manager hadn't intervened, the argument ___ into a real fight.
explanation: 'Might have + past participle' expresses an uncertain past outcome in a third conditional. The distractors use impossible forms after 'might have': bare infinitive, past tense 'had', and -ing.

title: Third conditional: contracted form
question: If we'd arrived earlier, we ___ seats near the front.
explanation: 'We'd' contracts 'we had'. The result clause uses 'would have + past participle', which contracts to 'would've got'. 'Would got' is grammatically incomplete.

title: Third conditional: regret with if only
question: ___ I'd checked the weather forecast, I wouldn't have left my umbrella at home.
explanation: 'If only + past perfect' expresses regret about a past situation, similar to a third conditional. The distractors are not grammatical English constructions.

title: Third conditional: couldn't have
question: If the traffic hadn't been so bad, we ___ the flight. But it was terrible.
explanation: 'Couldn't have missed' means it would have been impossible to miss the flight. This deduction about an unreal past uses 'couldn't have + past participle'.

title: Third conditional: mixed regret
question: She ___ the job if she'd prepared better for the interview. But she didn't, and she didn't get it.
explanation: The result clause of the third conditional requires 'would have + past participle'. The distractors use impossible forms: 'has' after a modal, -ing after 'have', and 'had have' (double auxiliary).

title: Third conditional: question with might
question: Do you think he ___ if we had invited him to the party last weekend?
explanation: 'Might have + past participle' expresses uncertainty about an unreal past outcome. The distractors use impossible forms: past tense 'came' instead of the participle 'come', 'had' after a modal, and -ing after 'have'.

title: Third conditional: negative could have
question: If she'd seen the sign, she ___ the wrong turn.
explanation: 'Couldn't have + past participle' means it would have been impossible. The distractors use impossible forms: past tense 'took' instead of the participle, 'had' after a modal, and -ing after 'have'.

title: Third conditional: would never have
question: If he hadn't moved abroad back in 2005, he ___ his wife.
explanation: 'Would never have + past participle' is the negative third conditional result. The distractors use impossible forms after 'have': bare infinitive, 'had' after a modal, and -ing.

title: Third conditional: past perfect if-clause
question: If I ___ the answer at the time, I would have told you immediately.
explanation: The if-clause of the third conditional uses 'had + past participle'. The distractors use impossible forms: 'had knew' (past tense after 'had'), 'would have known' (result-clause form in the if-clause), and 'had been knew' (nonsense passive).

title: Wish: past vs past perfect
question: I failed the test. I wish I ___ harder.
explanation: Regret about a specific completed past event requires 'wish + past perfect' (had + past participle). The distractors use impossible verb forms after 'wish'.

title: Wish: wish vs if only (present regret)
question: ___ I could turn back time and study languages as a child!
explanation: 'If only' is a stronger, more emotional version of 'I wish'. Both take the past simple for present unreal wishes. 'Only if' means 'on the condition that'.

title: Wish: would for complaints
question: I wish my neighbours ___ playing loud music. I've asked them so many times but they refuse.
explanation: 'Wish + would + base form' is used to complain about other people's annoying habits. 'Would stopped' mixes modal with past tense (impossible). 'Will stop' and 'are stopping' are ungrammatical after 'wish'.

title: Wish: wouldn't for irritation
question: I wish the neighbours ___ their dog out so late every night. It keeps waking me up.
explanation: 'Wish + wouldn't + base form' complains about repeated habits. 'Don't let' (present), 'wouldn't letting' (wrong form after modal), and 'won't let' (future) are all ungrammatical after 'wish'.

title: Wish: past perfect for deep regret
question: I wish I ___ that embarrassing email to my boss yesterday.
explanation: Regret about a specific past action requires 'wish + past perfect'. 'Hadn't sent' expresses the wish to undo a completed action.

title: Wish: if only + past perfect
question: If only we ___ the house when it was cheap!
explanation: 'If only + past perfect (had + past participle)' expresses strong regret about a past decision. The distractors use impossible verb forms.

title: Wish: distinguishing wish + would
question: Which sentence correctly uses 'wish + would'?
correct: I wish the bus would come on time for once.
wrong1: I wish I would be taller.
wrong2: I wish it would rained yesterday.
wrong3: I wish she would knew the answer.
explanation: 'Wish + would' is for complaints about others' behaviour or external situations. We don't say 'I wish I would' about ourselves, and 'would' is followed by the base form, not past tense.

title: Wish: could for ability
question: I wish I ___ speak Chinese. It would help at work.
explanation: 'Wish + could' expresses a desire for an ability you don't have. 'Wish + can' is grammatically incorrect. 'Wish + would' is for complaints, not abilities.

title: Wish: about the future
question: I wish tomorrow ___ a holiday, but unfortunately it's not.
explanation: For wishes about an unreal present/near-future state, use the past subjunctive. 'Were' (not 'was') is the subjunctive form. 'Will be', 'is', and 'has been' are all ungrammatical after 'wish' in this sense.

title: Wish: if only with would
question: If only the government ___ more about climate change! I keep writing to my MP but nothing happens.
explanation: 'If only + would + base form' expresses frustration about an ongoing situation. 'Would did' mixes modal with past tense, 'does' and 'is doing' are present forms that are ungrammatical after 'if only' in this sense.

title: Modals of deduction: must have vs had to
question: Look at her red eyes. She ___ crying before we arrived.
explanation: 'Must have + past participle' is a deduction about the past based on present evidence. The distractors use impossible verb forms (missing 'have', wrong participle, or doubled auxiliary).

title: Modals of deduction: can't have (past)
question: She ___ the message. Her phone was switched off all day.
explanation: 'Can't have + past participle' expresses certainty that something didn't happen in the past. The distractors use impossible verb forms (missing 'have', missing participle ending, or wrong -ing form).

title: Modals of deduction: must vs can't (present)
question: The lights are on and I can hear music. Someone ___ home.
explanation: 'Must be' expresses a strong present deduction based on evidence. The distractors use impossible forms: modals are not followed by -ing or 'to', and modals do not take -s.

title: Modals of deduction: couldn't have
question: He ___ the whole cake by himself. It was enormous!
explanation: 'Couldn't have + past participle' expresses disbelief about a past event. The distractors use the wrong participle form ('ate' not 'eaten'), wrong auxiliary agreement ('has' after modal), or -ing form after 'have'.

title: Modals of deduction: may have vs must have
question: She's not answering her phone. She ___ asleep, but I'm not sure.
explanation: 'May have + past participle' expresses possibility about a past event. The distractors use impossible forms: missing 'have', wrong past tense instead of participle, or -ing instead of participle.

title: Modals of deduction: must have vs had to (tricky)
question: The exam was compulsory. Every student ___ take it.
explanation: 'Had to' is the past tense of 'must' for obligations. 'Must have' + base form is ungrammatical (needs past participle), 'have to' produces a subject-verb agreement error after 'Every student', and 'musted' is not a real word.

title: Modals of deduction: can't have vs couldn't
question: He ___ been at the meeting yesterday. I saw him at the airport.
explanation: 'Can't have + past participle' deduces it was impossible he attended. The distractors use impossible auxiliary forms: 'can't had' (modal + past), 'couldn't been' (missing 'have'), 'can't having' (wrong form).

title: Modals of deduction: might have
question: Where's my wallet? I ___ it at the restaurant.
explanation: 'Might have + past participle' expresses possibility about a past event. The distractors use impossible forms: missing 'have', base form instead of participle, or -ing after 'might'.

title: Modals of deduction: present must be
question: You've been working all day without eating. You ___ starving!
explanation: 'Must be' makes a present deduction about a current state based on evidence. The distractors use impossible forms: 'must been' is missing 'have', modals do not take -s, and 'musts being' is doubly wrong.

title: Modals of deduction: can't be (present)
question: That ___ Sarah at the door. She's on holiday in Japan.
explanation: 'Can't be' expresses a strong present deduction that something is impossible. The distractors use impossible forms: 'can't being' (wrong form after modal), 'can't to be' ('to' after modal), 'cans't be' (nonword).

title: Relative clauses: that vs which
question: The book ___ I borrowed from the library was fascinating.
explanation: In a defining relative clause identifying which specific book, 'that' is preferred. 'Who' and 'whom' are for people, not things. 'Whose' shows possession, which doesn't fit here.

title: Relative clauses: non-defining with which
question: My car, ___ I bought last year, has already broken down twice.
explanation: Non-defining relative clauses (with commas) require 'which', not 'that'. The clause adds extra information about a car already identified as 'my car'.

title: Relative clauses: whose vs of which
question: That's the company ___ CEO resigned last week.
explanation: 'Whose' shows possession for both people and things. 'Who's' means 'who is'. 'Of which the CEO' is possible but very formal; 'whose' is the natural choice.

title: Relative clauses: where vs in which
question: The hotel ___ we stayed was right on the beach.
explanation: 'Where' replaces 'in/at which' for places. 'In where' is redundant since 'where' already contains the preposition meaning. 'That' would need 'that we stayed in'.

title: Relative clauses: that restriction
question: My sister, ___ lives in Berlin, is visiting next month.
explanation: In non-defining relative clauses (with commas), 'that' cannot be used. You must use 'who' for people. This clause adds extra info about an already-identified person.

title: Relative clauses: in which (formal)
question: 2020 was the year ___ everything changed.
explanation: 'In which' is the formal equivalent of 'when' for time expressions. 'Which' alone is incomplete; you'd need 'which everything changed in'. 'Where at' is non-standard.

title: Relative clauses: whose for things
question: I found a tree ___ branches had been struck by lightning.
explanation: 'Whose' works for both people and things to show possession. 'That its' is not grammatically valid as a relative pronoun construction.

title: Relative clauses: whom (formal)
question: The professor ___ I spoke to recommended this textbook.
explanation: 'Whom' is the object form, correct here because the professor receives the action (I spoke to the professor). In formal writing, 'to whom I spoke' is also used.

title: Relative clauses: omitting the pronoun
question: Is this the film ___ you were telling me about?
explanation: When the relative pronoun is the object of the clause, it can be omitted entirely in defining clauses. 'Which it' adds an unnecessary extra pronoun.

title: Relative clauses: non-defining with where
question: I went back to Edinburgh, ___ I was born.
explanation: Non-defining clauses about places use 'where' (set off by commas). 'That' cannot be used in non-defining clauses. 'Which place' is not a relative pronoun.

title: So/such...that: so + adjective
question: The suitcase was ___ heavy that I couldn't lift it.
explanation: 'So + adjective + that' is the correct pattern. 'Such' requires a noun phrase. 'Very' doesn't connect to the 'that' result clause.

title: So/such...that: such + a + noun
question: It was ___ difficult exam that half the class failed.
explanation: 'Such a + adjective + singular noun + that' is required. 'So a' is not grammatically valid. 'Such' without 'a' would need a plural or uncountable noun.

title: So/such...that: so + many
question: There were ___ many people that we couldn't find a seat.
explanation: 'So + many/much/few/little + that' is correct. 'Such' is not used before many/much/few/little. 'So much' doesn't collocate with the countable noun 'people'.

title: So/such...that: so + much (uncountable)
question: He had ___ much work that he stayed at the office until midnight.
explanation: 'So + much + uncountable noun + that' is the correct pattern. 'So many' is used with countable nouns, not 'work' which is uncountable.

title: So/such...that: such + plural noun
question: They were ___ rude customers that the waiter complained to his manager.
explanation: 'Such + adjective + plural noun + that' is correct. 'Such a' is only for singular nouns. 'So' needs an adjective alone, not followed by a noun.

title: So/such...that: so + few
question: There were ___ few options that we had to accept the first offer.
explanation: 'So + few + countable noun + that' is the correct pattern. 'Such few' is not standard. 'So little' is for uncountable nouns.

title: So/such...that: so + little (uncountable)
question: We had ___ little time that we couldn't visit the museum.
explanation: 'So + little + uncountable noun + that' is correct. 'So few' is for countable nouns. 'Such a little' changes the meaning to 'a small amount of'.

title: So/such...that: such + uncountable noun
question: It was ___ terrible weather that the match was cancelled.
explanation: 'Such + adjective + uncountable noun + that' is correct. 'Such a' cannot precede uncountable nouns. 'So' would need the adjective alone: 'so terrible that...'.

title: So/such...that: distinguishing so from such
question: She spoke ___ quickly that nobody could understand her.
explanation: 'So + adverb + that' is the correct pattern. 'Such' must precede a noun phrase, but 'quickly' is an adverb, not a noun.

title: So/such...that: such a + positive meaning
question: It was ___ good film that we watched it twice.
explanation: 'Such a + adjective + singular countable noun + that' is the correct pattern. 'So' would work with just the adjective: 'so good that...', but not before the noun 'film'.
